questions.stem: For each clinical finding, click to specify whether the finding supports heparin-induced thrombocytopenia or is less supportive of that diagnosis.
questions.scenario: The client has been receiving IV heparin for 6 days after surgery.

options.text: Supports HIT
Less supportive of HIT

matrixRows.label: Platelet count drops by more than 50%
Stable platelet trend with isolated bruising at IV sites

questions.rationale: HIT is recognized faster when the nurse stops focusing on bleeding alone and pairs thrombocytopenia with thrombosis clues after recent heparin exposure. Platelet drop plus new thrombosis pattern is more convincing than coagulation numbers alone.

distractorRationales.a: This option makes Supports HIT the focus, without accounting for Think HIT when platelet fall and thrombosis show up together after heparin exposure. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so the row-specific answer pattern better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.b: This distractor pulls attention toward Less supportive of HIT, when the bedside priority is driven by Think HIT when platelet fall and thrombosis show up together after heparin exposure. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The correct path is the row-specific answer pattern, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.

questions.takeaway: Think HIT when platelet fall and thrombosis show up together after heparin exposure.
questions.speedCue: The exam shortcut is thrombocytopenia plus clotting, not thrombocytopenia plus bleeding only.

questions.stem: For each client finding, click to specify whether the finding is more consistent with neuroleptic malignant syndrome or less consistent with neuroleptic malignant syndrome.
questions.scenario: The client recently started a high-potency antipsychotic and now has fever and confusion.

options.text: More consistent with NMS
Less consistent with NMS

questions.rationale: NMS is easier to recognize when the learner locks onto severe rigidity and autonomic instability after dopamine-blocking medication exposure. Hyperreflexia and diarrhea push the pattern away from NMS and more toward serotonin syndrome.

distractorRationales.a: Selecting More consistent with NMS would be unsafe, rather than acting on NMS is easier to recognize when the learner locks onto severe rigidity and autonomic instability after dopamine-blocking medication. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of infection progression and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.b: This distractor pulls attention toward Less consistent with NMS, but the stem cue is Rigidity after antipsychotic exposure should move NMS higher than serotonin syndrome. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so It is less defensible because the row-specific answer pattern targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.

questions.takeaway: Rigidity after antipsychotic exposure should move NMS higher than serotonin syndrome.
questions.speedCue: When a high-fever medication question includes rigidity, slow down and separate rigidity from hyperreflexia.

visualRationale.title: neuroleptic_malignant_syndrome
visualRationale.caption: Rigidity after antipsychotic exposure should move NMS higher than serotonin syndrome.
visualRationale.conclusion: Rigidity after antipsychotic exposure should move NMS higher than serotonin syndrome.

questions.stem: A client develops wheezing, facial swelling, and hypotension during an IV antibiotic infusion. Place the nursing actions in the order the nurse should take them.
questions.scenario: The client is anxious and struggling to speak in full sentences.

options.text: Stop the infusion and call for immediate help
Administer epinephrine per protocol
Support airway and oxygenation
Prepare for additional fluids and monitoring after first-line treatment

questions.rationale: Anaphylaxis becomes easier to answer when the nurse spots the airway-perfusion emergency pattern and moves rapidly. Stop the trigger, support oxygenation, give epinephrine, and then continue the resuscitation bundle with fluids and monitoring.
questions.takeaway: Anaphylaxis is a trigger-off, airway-support, epinephrine-first sequence.
questions.speedCue: Wheeze plus swelling plus hypotension should make you stop the medication and think epinephrine, not antihistamine-only care.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client with a T4 spinal cord injury develops a pounding headache, flushing above the injury, and severe hypertension. Place the actions in the order the nurse should take them.
questions.scenario: The urinary drainage tubing is kinked at the bedside.

options.text: Sit the client upright
Check and relieve the noxious trigger such as the bladder problem
Call for rapid provider support if blood pressure remains high
Prepare antihypertensive rescue therapy if ordered after trigger relief

questions.rationale: Autonomic dysreflexia is managed by lowering pressure with positioning, removing the trigger, escalating if pressure does not improve, and preparing medication support as needed. The bladder clue is the speed clue that should shorten decision time.
questions.takeaway: Severe hypertension in high spinal injury means sit up, find the trigger, and relieve it fast.
questions.speedCue: The fast clue is headache plus flushing plus hypertension above the lesion, especially when a bladder trigger is present.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client one hour after delivery has heavy bleeding and a boggy uterus. Place the nursing actions in the order the nurse should take them.
questions.scenario: The pad is saturated and the client reports dizziness while holding the newborn.

options.text: Massage the fundus
Call for help and bring hemorrhage support to the bedside
Assess bladder fullness and assist with emptying if needed
Prepare uterotonic medication after initial bedside measures

questions.rationale: Heavy postpartum bleeding with a boggy uterus is managed as a hemorrhage problem first. The nurse brings help, massages the fundus, corrects bladder-related atony contributors, and prepares uterotonic support without waiting for routine reassessment.
questions.takeaway: Boggy uterus means act on atony now, not after more observation.
questions.speedCue: On exam day, boggy plus bleeding should push uterine atony to the top immediately.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client 2 weeks after kidney transplant has a temperature of 100.8 F (38.2 C), tenderness over the graft site, and decreased urine output. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Notify the provider because these findings may indicate acute rejection.
Reassure the client that mild fever is expected after transplant.
Instruct the client to skip the next immunosuppressant dose.
Delay action until the next routine clinic visit.

questions.rationale: Fever, graft tenderness, and decreased urine output after kidney transplant are concerning for acute rejection. The nurse should escalate promptly for assessment and intervention. Missed or delayed response can threaten graft survival.

distractorRationales.b: This answer choice treats Reassure the client that mild fever is expected after transplant as the key issue, rather than acting on A client 2 weeks after kidney transplant has a temperature of 100. Normalizes a finding that should change the nurse's priority, so The nurse should prioritize Notify the provider because these findings may indicate acute rejection; otherwise infection progression remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.c: The reasoning trap in Instruct the client to skip the next immunosuppressant dose is that it moves to teaching or reassurance before the immediate risk is stabilized, when the bedside priority is driven by decreased urine output. That choice could make the nurse miss the window for infection or sepsis escalation while infection progression progresses.
distractorRationales.d: This choice focuses on Delay action until the next routine clinic visit, while the decisive cue is A client 2 weeks after kidney transplant has a temperature of 100. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so The nurse should prioritize Notify the provider because these findings may indicate acute rejection; otherwise infection progression remains the active threat.

questions.takeaway: Graft tenderness with falling urine output after transplant is a report-now pattern.

questions.stem: During labor a client suddenly becomes short of breath, cyanotic, and hypotensive. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Call for immediate emergency support and begin high-flow oxygen.
Reposition the client and wait to see if the symptoms pass.
Obtain a routine pain score before intervening.
Document the event as an anxiety reaction.

questions.rationale: Sudden hypoxia and hypotension during labor raise concern for amniotic fluid embolism, a catastrophic obstetric emergency. The nurse should escalate immediately and support oxygenation and circulation. Delay can rapidly endanger both the client and fetus.

distractorRationales.b: The problem with Reposition the client and wait to see if the symptoms pass is that it treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, rather than acting on During labor a client suddenly becomes short of breath, cyanotic. The safer answer is Call for immediate emergency support and begin high-flow oxygen because delaying airway and breathing intervention can worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Selecting Obtain a routine pain score before intervening would be unsafe, although the nurse should be guided by The nurse should escalate immediately and support oxygenation and circulation. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so Choosing it can delay airway and breathing intervention and leave oxygenation or ventilation unresolved.
distractorRationales.d: This option makes Document the event as an anxiety reaction the focus, and misses that Sudden hypoxia and hypotension during labor raise concern for amniotic fluid embolism, a catastrophic obstetric emergency. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Call for immediate emergency support and begin high-flow oxygen is safer because it addresses oxygenation or ventilation before routine care or reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Sudden maternal collapse in labor requires immediate emergency response and oxygenation support.

questions.stem: A client rescued from a house fire has singed nasal hair, soot around the mouth, and new hoarseness. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Prepare for early airway support because edema can rapidly worsen.
Apply topical burn cream to the face before any other action.
Encourage the client to drink water to soothe the throat.
Document the findings and reassess at the end of the shift.

questions.rationale: Hoarseness, soot exposure, and singed nasal hair suggest inhalation injury with potential airway edema. The nurse should prioritize airway protection early rather than waiting for visible obstruction. Delayed action can make intubation much more difficult once swelling progresses.

distractorRationales.b: This option overweights Apply topical burn cream to the face before any other action, rather than acting on Smoke inhalation clues plus hoarseness mean protect the airway early. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The priority is Prepare for early airway support because edema can rapidly worsen, not a step that delays stabilization of oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Encourage the client to drink water to soothe the throat would not address the priority, without accounting for Smoke inhalation clues plus hoarseness mean protect the airway early. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so That choice could make the nurse miss the window for airway and breathing intervention while oxygenation or ventilation progresses.
distractorRationales.d: The problem with Document the findings and reassess at the end of the shift is that it treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, despite the clinical warning in Smoke inhalation clues plus hoarseness mean protect the airway early. The safer answer is Prepare for early airway support because edema can rapidly worsen because delaying airway and breathing intervention can worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Smoke inhalation clues plus hoarseness mean protect the airway early.

questions.stem: After rupture of membranes, the fetal heart rate suddenly drops to 70/min and the nurse sees a loop of umbilical cord at the vaginal opening. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Use a gloved hand to lift the presenting part and position the client to relieve cord compression.
Push the cord back into the vagina.
Apply internal fetal scalp monitoring.
Delay action until the provider arrives.

questions.rationale: Visible cord with acute fetal bradycardia indicates umbilical cord prolapse, an obstetric emergency. The nurse should immediately relieve pressure on the cord by lifting the presenting part and repositioning the client while calling for help. Handling the cord unnecessarily or waiting increases fetal hypoxia risk.

distractorRationales.b: The reasoning trap in Push the cord back into the vagina is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, when the bedside priority is driven by Handling the cord unnecessarily or waiting increases fetal hypoxia risk. The correct path is Use a gloved hand to lift the presenting part and position the client to relieve cord, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.c: The reasoning trap in Apply internal fetal scalp monitoring is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, and misses that Handling the cord unnecessarily or waiting increases fetal hypoxia risk. The correct path is Use a gloved hand to lift the presenting part and position the client to relieve cord, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.d: Delay action until the provider arrives is not the best answer, and misses that Handling the cord unnecessarily or waiting increases fetal hypoxia risk. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so Use a gloved hand to lift the presenting part and position the client to relieve cord is safer because it addresses circulation or perfusion before routine care or reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Cord prolapse is a relieve-the-compression-now emergency.

questions.stem: A client with a positive pregnancy test reports sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain, dizziness, and shoulder pain. Blood pressure is 84/46 mmHg. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Notify the provider and prepare for emergency management of a ruptured ectopic pregnancy.
Place the client on strict bed rest and reassess in 30 minutes.
Prepare the client for a routine pelvic examination.
Tell the client the discomfort is expected in early pregnancy.

questions.rationale: Severe unilateral pain, hypotension, dizziness, and referred shoulder pain suggest rupture with internal bleeding from an ectopic pregnancy. The nurse should treat this as a hemorrhagic emergency and escalate immediately. Delay increases the risk of shock and maternal collapse.

distractorRationales.b: The clinical error in Place the client on strict bed rest and reassess in 30 minutes is that it treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, without accounting for Unstable pain and bleeding clues in early pregnancy should trigger immediate concern for ruptured ectopic pregnancy. Notify the provider and prepare for emergency management of a ruptured ectopic pregnancy is safer because it addresses bleeding or anticoagulation injury before routine care or reassurance.
distractorRationales.c: This choice focuses on Prepare the client for a routine pelvic examination, rather than acting on referred shoulder pain suggest rupture with internal bleeding from an ectopic pregnancy. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so The safer answer is Notify the provider and prepare for emergency management of a ruptured ectopic pregnancy because delaying bleeding-risk intervention can worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor pulls attention toward Tell the client the discomfort is expected in early pregnancy, when the bedside priority is driven by Severe unilateral pain, hypotension, dizziness. Normalizes a finding that should change the nurse's priority, so Notify the provider and prepare for emergency management of a ruptured ectopic pregnancy is safer because it addresses bleeding or anticoagulation injury before routine care or reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Unstable pain and bleeding clues in early pregnancy should trigger immediate concern for ruptured ectopic pregnancy.

questions.stem: An infant has sudden episodes of crying with knees drawn to the chest and passes a stool that looks like currant jelly. Which action should the nurse anticipate first?

options.text: Prepare the child for urgent evaluation and possible air enema reduction.
Give an antidiarrheal and observe for improvement.
Encourage the parent to offer more formula at home.
Document the stool as a common viral finding.

questions.rationale: Colicky pain with knees drawn up and currant jelly stool strongly suggests intussusception. Prompt evaluation is needed because bowel ischemia can develop if the obstruction is not reduced. Supportive home measures do not address the underlying emergency.

distractorRationales.b: Give an antidiarrheal and observe for improvement would not address the priority, when the bedside priority is driven by Colicky pain with knees drawn up and currant jelly stool strongly suggests intussusception. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so It is less defensible because Prepare the child for urgent evaluation and possible air enema reduction targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.
distractorRationales.c: The problem with Encourage the parent to offer more formula at home is that it moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, despite the clinical warning in An infant has sudden episodes of crying with knees drawn to the chest and passes a stool. Choosing it can delay circulation and perfusion support and leave circulation or perfusion unresolved.
distractorRationales.d: Document the stool as a common viral finding is not the best answer, and misses that Colicky pain with knees drawn up and currant jelly stool strongly suggests intussusception. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so It is less defensible because Prepare the child for urgent evaluation and possible air enema reduction targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.

questions.takeaway: Currant jelly stool plus episodic pain is a pattern clue for intussusception, not simple gastroenteritis.

questions.stem: A 12-hour-old newborn is lethargic, feeds poorly, and has a temperature of 97.1 F (36.2 C). Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Notify the provider because these findings suggest neonatal sepsis.
Reassure the parent that the newborn is just sleepy.
Delay evaluation until the next routine feeding.
Place the newborn prone for a deeper sleep.

questions.rationale: Poor feeding, lethargy, and temperature instability in a newborn are concerning for sepsis and require prompt evaluation. Neonates often show subtle signs rather than high fever. Waiting can delay lifesaving treatment in a high-risk age group.

distractorRationales.b: This answer choice treats Reassure the parent that the newborn is just sleepy as the key issue, instead of responding to temperature instability in a newborn are concerning for sepsis and require prompt evaluation. Normalizes a finding that should change the nurse's priority, so The nurse should prioritize Notify the provider because these findings suggest neonatal sepsis; otherwise infection progression remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.c: This option overweights Delay evaluation until the next routine feeding, instead of responding to has a temperature of 97. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so The safer answer is Notify the provider because these findings suggest neonatal sepsis because delaying infection or sepsis escalation can worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.d: The reasoning trap in Place the newborn prone for a deeper sleep is that it normalizes a finding that should change the nurse's priority, and misses that A 12-hour-old newborn is lethargic, feeds poorly. It is less defensible because Notify the provider because these findings suggest neonatal sepsis targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.

questions.takeaway: Subtle instability in a newborn should be taken seriously because sepsis can present quietly.
